content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 1, 1990

Senator H. L. Richardson, Ret.
Chairman, Gun Owners of California
Campaign Committee
3440 Viking Drive, Suite 106
Sacramento, CA 95827

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-651

Dear Senator Richardson:

You have requested advice under the Proposition 73 provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Because your request
is a general inquiry, we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy
enclosed).2/

QUESTIONS

1. May Gun Owners of California Campaign Committee ("Gun
Owners"), a broad based political committee, receive contributions
from candidate-controlled campaign committees for the purpose of
making independent expenditures on behalf of Assembly candidate
David Knowles?

2. As part of its efforts to support state Assembly
candidate David Knowles, may Gun Owners make contributions to
other broad based political committees for the purpose of making
contributions to the David Knowles campaign committee?

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83113; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-90-651
Page 2


     3.  According to Gun Owners' review of recently filed
campaign statements, state Assembly democratic candidates'
campaign committees transferred approximately $660,528 in campaign
funds to the state central democratic committee. Assembly
candidate Norm Waters' campaign statements disclosed receipt of a
contribution in the amount of $5,000 from the state central
democratic committee. Is the transfer of this money a violation
of Proposition 73?

     4.  According to Gun Owners' review of recently filed
campaign statements, California Trial Lawyers Association ("CTLA")
made a contribution to Assembly candidate Norm Waters in the
amount of $5,000. CTLA also had made contributions totaling
$100,000 to the state central democratic committee, which had
made, in turn, a contribution to Assembly candidate Waters. Is
the transfer of this money a violation of Proposition 73?

CONCLUSION

     1.  Gun Owners may not receive campaign funds from candidates
and their controlled committees for the purpose of making
independent expenditures to state Assembly candidate David
Knowles.

     2.  Gun Owners may not make "earmarked" contributions to
other broad based political committees for the purpose of making
contributions in excess of the limits to state Assembly candidate
David Knowles.

     3. and 4.  The Commission does not provide advice concerning
the past conduct of public officials. Furthermore, the Commission
does not provide advice to third parties concerning another
person's duties under the Act.

ANALYSIS

     As you know, Proposition 73's fiscal year limitations on
campaign contributions and prohibition on transfers between and
among candidates were ruled unconstitutional by a District
Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et
al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission (Case No. CIVS-89-0433
LKK-JFM) ("[SEIU]" September 25, 1990. Sections 85301, 85302,
and 85303(a) and (b) placed limitations on campaign contributions
based on a fiscal year regulatory scheme.3/ Section 85304
prohibited candidates and their controlled committees from

3/ Section 85302 limited campaign contributions to political
committees to $2,500 in a fiscal year. Sections 85301 and 85303
provided that a "person" could contribute up to $1,000 in a fiscal
year to a candidate; a "political committee" could contribute up
to $2,500 in a fiscal year to a candidate; and a "broad based
political committee or political party" could contribute up to
$5,000 in a fiscal year to a candidate.

Our File No. I-90-651
Page 3


transferring campaign funds to other candidates.4/  While these
sections were invalidated by the court's decision in the SEIU
case, under a stay issued by the federal district court on
September 28, 1990, these sections continue to remain valid and
enforceable with respect to legislative candidates and local
candidates who are subject to valid campaign contribution
limitations.  However, Section 85304 no longer restricts the
ability of a legislative candidate to transfer campaign funds to
statewide candidates or to local candidates not subject to
contribution limits.

QUESTION NO. 1:

As stated in your letter, the ultimate beneficiary of the
independent expenditure efforts of Gun Owners is a legislative
candidate.  Therefore, Proposition 73's fiscal year contribution
limitations and ban on transfers are still applicable.  Clearly,
if the contributor to Gun Owners is a committee controlled by a
legislative candidate (or a committee controlled by a local
candidate who is subject to valid contribution limits), Gun Owners
cannot use campaign funds received from such a source to make
either a contribution or an independent expenditure to a
legislative candidate (or local candidate subject to contribution
limits).5/  Such a transaction would be a violation of Proposition
73's transfer ban.  Gun Owners would have to segregate such
contributions upon receipt by placing them into a separate account
in order to avoid tainting the committee's existing candidate
contribution and administrative accounts.6/

For the same reason, if the contributor is any other type of
candidate, for example, a statewide candidate or local candidate
who is not subject to contribution limitations, and the ultimate
beneficiary is a legislative or local candidate subject to valid
contribution limits, the transfer of their campaign funds to such
a regulated candidate through Gun Owners also is prohibited.

_______________________
4/   Section 85304 provides in pertinent part:

     No candidate for elective office or committee
     controlled by that candidate or candidates for
     elective office shall transfer any contribution to
     any other candidate for elective office.

5/   However, Gun Owners can use campaign funds received from
non-candidate-controlled committees for non-candidate related
activities.  (Section 85303(c); see generally, Bagatelos Advice
Letter, No. I-89-327; Martin Advice Letter, No. A-89-301; copies
enclosed.)

6/   We assume that Gun Owners uses its administrative account for
the purpose of making independent expenditures to legislative or
local candidates subject to contribution limits.

Our File No. I-90-651
Page 4


Candidates and their controlled committees cannot do indirectly,
through a political committee, what they cannot do directly, which
is to transfer their campaign funds to a regulated candidate in
violation of Section 85304.

Gun Owners may accept contributions from candidate-controlled
committees.77/ However, Gun Owners is barred from making
contributions or independent expenditures to legislative candidate
David Knowles with campaign funds from such candidate-controlled
committees.

QUESTION NO. 2:

In your second inquiry, you pose a slightly different slant
to the same issue. In this situation, Gun Owners could be the
contributor. The recipients of Gun Owners' contributions would be
other broad based political committees. Further, since Gun Owners
has "earmarked" its contributions to be used for making a
contribution to a legislative candidate, the ultimate beneficiary
of Gun Owners' contributions would be the legislative candidate
David Knowles. Assuming the recipient broad based political
committee made the contribution to Knowles pursuant to your
directions, it would be acting as an intermediary on Gun Owners'
behalf. (Contributions by an intermediary or agent are prohibited
under the Act unless full disclosure is made to the recipient
candidate of the campaign contribution, in this case, David Knowles. (See Section 84302.) Assuming further that Gun Owners
has already made a $5,000 contribution to David Knowles during the
fiscal year, additional efforts to contribute to this particular
legislative candidate in this manner would be
proscribed under Proposition 73.

Gun Owners may make contributions, which are not "earmarked,"
to other broad based political committees. These contributions
can be used for all purposes, including making contributions or
independent expenditures to legislative candidates or local
candidates who are subject to valid contribution limitations,
provided the contributions made by Gun Owners originate from
accounts which do not contain any campaign funds from candidates
or their controlled committees.

QUESTIONS 3 and 4:

The Act requires the Commission to provide formal written
advice to any person whose duties under the Act are in question or
to that person's authorized representative. (Section 83114(b).)
The Commission does not provide advice to third parties concerning
another person's duties under the Act. (Regulation 18329(b)(8).)

77/ This assumes that a candidate-controlled committee's
expenditure of campaign funds to Gun Owners for this purpose is
permissible under the trust provisions of the Act, Sections
85202(b) and 85801.

Our File No. I-90-651
Page 5


Since you are not requesting advice on behalf of State Assemblyman
Norm Waters or the State Democratic Central Committee, we are
unable to provide advice on the issues raised in your letter.

In addition, please note that the Commission does not provide
advice concerning past conduct of public officials (Regulation
18329(b)(8).) Complaints about possible violations of the Act are
handled by our Enforcement Division. If you have any questions
about filing a complaint, please contact the Enforcement Division
at (916) 322-6441.

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[illegible]

By: Deanne Stone
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:DS:aa

Enclosures

2. As part of its efforts to support state Assembly candidate David Knowles, may Gun Owners make contributions to other broad based political committees for the purpose of making contributions to the David Knowles campaign committee?

1, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83113; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

3. Assembly candidate Norm Waters' campaign statements disclosed receipt of a contribution in the amount of $5,000 from the state central democratic committee. Is the transfer of this money a violation of Proposition 73?

4. According to Gun Owners' review of recently filed campaign statements, California Trial Lawyers Association ("CTLA") made a contribution to Assembly candidate Norm Waters in the amount of $5,000. CTLA also had made contributions totaling $100,000 to the state central democratic committee, which had made, in turn, a contribution to Assembly candidate Waters. Is the transfer of this money a violation of Proposition 73?
sections.conclusion:
1. Gun Owners may not receive campaign funds from candidates and their controlled committees for the purpose of making independent expenditures to state Assembly candidate David Knowles.

2. Gun Owners may not make "earmarked" contributions to other broad based political committees for the purpose of making contributions in excess of the limits to state Assembly candidate David Knowles.

3. and 4. The Commission does not provide advice concerning the past conduct of public officials. Furthermore, the Commission does not provide advice to third parties concerning another person's duties under the Act.
sections.analysis:
As you know, Proposition 73's fiscal year limitations on campaign contributions and prohibition on transfers between and among candidates were ruled unconstitutional by a U.S. District Court judge in Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission (Case No. CIVS-89-0433 LKK-JFM) ("SEIU") on September 25, 1990. Sections 85301, 85302, and 85303(a) and (b) placed limitations on campaign contributions based on a fiscal year regulatory scheme.3/ Section 85304 prohibited candidates and their controlled committees from

3/ Section 85302 limited campaign contributions to political committees to $2,500 in a fiscal year. Sections 85301 and 85303 provided that a "person" could contribute up to $1,000 in a fiscal year to a candidate; a "political committee" could contribute up to $2,500 in a fiscal year to a candidate; and a "broad based political committee or political party" could contribute up to $5,000 in a fiscal year to a candidate.

transferring campaign funds to other candidates.4/ While these sections were invalidated by the court’s decision in the SEIU case, under a stay issued by the federal district court on September 28, 1990, these sections continue to remain valid and enforceable with respect to legislative candidates and local candidates who are subject to valid campaign contribution limitations. However, Section 85304 no longer restricts the ability of a legislative candidate to transfer campaign funds to statewide candidates or to local candidates not subject to contribution limits.

QUESTION NO. 1:

As stated in your letter, the ultimate beneficiary of the independent expenditure efforts of Gun Owners is a legislative candidate. Therefore, Proposition 73’s fiscal year contribution limitations and ban on transfers are still applicable. Clearly, if the contributor to Gun Owners is a committee controlled by a legislative candidate (or a committee controlled by a local candidate who is subject to valid contribution limits), Gun Owners cannot use campaign funds received from such a source to make either a contribution or an independent expenditure to a legislative candidate (or local candidate subject to contribution limits).5/ Such a transaction would be a violation of Proposition 73’s transfer ban. Gun Owners would have to segregate such contributions upon receipt by placing them into a separate account in order to avoid tainting the committee’s existing candidate contribution and administrative accounts.6/

For the same reason, if the contributor is any other type of candidate, for example, a statewide candidate or local candidate who is not subject to contribution limitations, and the ultimate beneficiary is a legislative or local candidate subject to valid contribution limits, the transfer of their campaign funds to such a regulated candidate through Gun Owners also is prohibited.

4/ Section 85304 provides in pertinent part:

No candidate for elective office or committee controlled by that candidate or candidates for elective office shall transfer any contribution to any other candidate for elective office.

5/ However, Gun Owners can use campaign funds received from candidate-controlled committees for non-candidate related activities. (Section 85303(c); see generally, Bagatelos Advice Letter, No. I-89-327; Martin Advice Letter, No. A-89-301; copies enclosed.)

6/ We assume that Gun Owners uses its administrative account for the purpose of making independent expenditures to legislative or local candidates subject to contribution limits.

Candidates and their controlled committees cannot do indirectly, through a political committee, what they cannot do directly, which is to transfer their campaign funds to a regulated candidate in violation of Section 85304.

Gun Owners may accept contributions from candidate-controlled committees.7/ However, Gun Owners is barred from making contributions or independent expenditures to legislative candidate David Knowles with campaign funds from such candidate-controlled committees.

QUESTION NO. 2:

In your second inquiry, you pose a slightly different slant to the same issue. In this situation, Gun Owners would be the contributor. The recipients of Gun Owners' contributions would be other broad based political committees. Further, since Gun Owners has "earmarked" its contributions to be used for making a contribution to a legislative candidate, the ultimate beneficiary of Gun Owners' contributions would be the legislative candidate David Knowles. Assuming the recipient broad based political committee made the contribution to Knowles pursuant to your directions, it would be acting as an intermediary on Gun Owners' behalf. (Section 84302.5.) Contributions by an intermediary or agent are prohibited under the Act unless full disclosure is made to the recipient candidate of the campaign contribution, in this case, David Knowles. (Section 84302.) Assuming further that Gun Owners already has made a $5,000 contribution to David Knowles during the fiscal year, additional efforts to contribute to this particular legislative candidate in this manner would be proscribed under Proposition 73.

Gun Owners may make contributions, which are not "earmarked," to other broad based political committees. These contributions can be used for all purposes, including making contributions or independent expenditures to legislative candidates or local candidates who are subject to valid contribution limitations, provided the contributions made by Gun Owners originate from accounts which do not contain any campaign funds from candidates or their controlled committees.

QUESTIONS 3 and 4:

The Act requires the Commission to provide formal written advice to any person whose duties under the Act are in question or to that person's authorized representative. (Section 83114(b).) The Commission does not provide advice to third parties concerning another person's duties under the Act. (Regulation 18329(b)(8).)

7/ This assumes that a candidate-controlled committee's expenditure of campaign funds to Gun Owners for this purpose is permissible under the trust provisions of the Act, Sections 85202(b) and 85801.

Since you are not requesting advice on behalf of State Assemblyman Norm Waters or the State Democratic Central Committee, we are unable to provide advice on the issues raised in your letter.

In addition, please note that the Commission does not provide advice concerning past conduct of public officials. (Regulation 18329(b)(8).) Complaints about possible violations of the Act are handled by our Enforcement Division. If you have any questions about filing a complaint, please contact the Enforcement Division at (916) 322-6441.

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you requested. If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.
